description: Figure 4 presents the tensile mechanical response of Fe–20Mn–9Al–1.5C–2Ni–3Cr low-density steel under different heat-treatment conditions. Panel (a) shows engineering stress–strain curves for four specimens: multi-stage heat treatment (MHT), single-stage austenitization at 1000 °C (S1000), 1050 °C (S1050), and 1100 °C (S1100). S1000 and S1050 exhibit the highest yield and ultimate tensile strengths, both exceeding 1100 MPa with total elongations above 55 %. MHT displays marginally lower strength yet retains high ductility near 50 %, while S1100 shows the lowest strength but the largest elongation. Panel (b) plots true stress (solid lines) and strain-hardening rate (dashed lines) against true strain. The work-hardening rate evolves through three marked stages: an initial sharp decrease (Stage I), a rise to a peak of approximately 2000 MPa near true strain 0.20–0.22 (Stage II), and a final decline (Stage III). MHT, S1000, and S1050 exhibit similar hardening capacities, whereas S1100 shows a distinctly lower rate. These curves demonstrate that the optimized multi-stage heat treatment preserves a strength–ductility profile comparable to the best single-stage conditions, supporting grain-boundary engineering that improves toughness without sacrificing tensile performance.
